Introduction to sovpal

library(sovpal)
has_ggplot2 <- requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)
has_jpeg    <- requireNamespace("jpeg", quietly = TRUE)

sovpal provides color palettes drawn from the Soviet world — GOST pipeline-identification standards, military paint specifications, and the palettes of avant-garde artworks. Colors are historically sourced, and many of them mean something: in GOST 14202-69, green is water, red is fire suppression, yellow is gas.

#> Warning: The following aesthetics were dropped during statistical transformation: fill.
#> ℹ This can happen when ggplot fails to infer the correct grouping structure in
#>   the data.
#> ℹ Did you forget to specify a `group` aesthetic or to convert a numerical
#>   variable into a factor?

Palettes by domain

Each palette has a thematic domain and a functional type (qualitative / sequential / diverging):

sovpal_palettes()
#>            palette     domain        type n_colors
#> 1        gost14202 industrial qualitative        8
#> 2      gost_signal industrial qualitative        4
#> 3  soviet_military   military qualitative        6
#> 4           steppe   military  sequential        3
#> 5        lissitzky   artistic qualitative        4
#> 6           popova   artistic qualitative        5
#> 7        stepanova   artistic qualitative        5
#> 8         malevich   artistic qualitative        5
#> 9           hazard  composite   diverging        3
#> 10     hazard_warm  composite   diverging        3
#> 11      hazard_cvd  composite   diverging        3
show_all_palettes()

Industrial — colors that mean something

The distinctive thing about the GOST palettes is semantic color. Because sovpal() returns a named vector, you can map categories to their correct colors with scale_*_manual() — water is always green, gas always yellow, regardless of plotting order.

set.seed(1969)
fluids <- names(sovpal("gost14202"))
flow <- expand.grid(year = 1965:1980, fluid = fluids)
flow$throughput <- round(abs(
  rep(c(120, 40, 70, 55, 25, 30, 90, 45), each = 16) +
    rep(seq(0, 30, length.out = 16), times = 8) +
    rnorm(nrow(flow), 0, 8)
))
flow$fluid <- factor(flow$fluid, levels = fluids)

ggplot(flow, aes(year, throughput, fill = fluid)) +
  geom_area(colour = "grey15", linewidth = 0.15) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = sovpal("gost14202")) +
  labs(title = "Pipeline throughput by fluid category",
       subtitle = "GOST 14202-69 colors mapped to their meanings",
       x = NULL, y = "Throughput", fill = "Fluid")

Military — paint and camouflage

soviet_military is an earth-toned set of period vehicle and aircraft colors; steppe (the hero image above) is the camouflage terrain ramp.

set.seed(42)
classes <- names(sovpal("soviet_military"))
dens <- data.frame(
  value = c(rnorm(200, 5), rnorm(200, 6.5), rnorm(200, 4),
            rnorm(200, 7), rnorm(200, 5.5), rnorm(200, 8)),
  class = factor(rep(classes, each = 200), levels = classes)
)
ggplot(dens, aes(value, fill = class)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.65, colour = "grey15", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_sovpal("soviet_military") +
  labs(title = "soviet_military", x = NULL, y = "Density", fill = NULL)

Artistic — palettes from the avant-garde

Each artistic palette is color-sampled from a specific artwork and named for the artist (cf. ). The hex values are approximate, sampled from reproductions — see data-raw/sample_artwork.R.

stepanova is sampled from Varvara Stepanova’s Billiard Players (1920) — a muted cubo-futurist work of charcoal, slate, brick, gold, and khaki. Her work is still under copyright in the EU (she died in 1958), so the image is not bundled; view it at the Museo Thyssen-Bornemisza.

show_palette("stepanova", notes = FALSE)

Composite — diverging scales

For a diverging scale the midpoint should be the lightest point. The composite diverging palettes use a light cream center; hazard_warm (green → cream → red) is the most legible:

v$centered <- v$elev - mean(v$elev)
lim <- max(abs(v$centered))
ggplot(v, aes(col, row, fill = centered)) +
  geom_raster() +
  scale_fill_sovpal("hazard_warm", discrete = FALSE,
                    limits = c(-lim, lim), name = "Δ elev") +
  coord_equal(expand = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "hazard_warm", subtitle = "green / cream / red") +
  theme(axis.title = element_blank(), axis.text = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank())

When red–green colorblindness matters, hazard_cvd (blue → cream → red) carries the same safe-to-danger meaning with distinguishable hues. The archival hazard (green / grey / red) keeps its faithful GOST values as the historical record.

Usage

# Retrieve a palette (named vector)
sovpal("malevich")
#>      navy       red      gold     green      rose 
#> "#161B49" "#B9592F" "#CEA829" "#5D8169" "#BB9E99"

# n colors, or interpolate a continuous ramp
sovpal("gost14202", n = 4)
#>     water      fire       air       gas 
#> "#2E7D32" "#C62828" "#5B8DB8" "#F9A825"
length(sovpal("steppe", n = 100, type = "continuous"))
#> [1] 100

# Slice or subset with base-R indexing
sovpal("gost14202")[c("water", "fire", "air")]
#>     water      fire       air 
#> "#2E7D32" "#C62828" "#5B8DB8"

palette_info() reports domain, provenance, intended use, colorblindness caveats, and computed white-background contrast (WCAG ratio; 3:1 is the non-text threshold). Lissitzky’s off-white ground is flagged automatically:

info <- palette_info("lissitzky")
info$source
#> [1] "Color-sampled from a reproduction of El Lissitzky, 'Beat the Whites with the Red Wedge' (1919). Approximate."
info$contrast_white
#> [1]  1.18  4.15 18.59  3.10
info$low_contrast_on_white
#> [1] "ground"

Historical notes

GOST 14202-69 — pipeline identification. Adopted in 1969, it standardized the color-coding of industrial pipelines across all Soviet enterprises: eight base colors for fluid categories, referenced by paint card number.

GOST 12.4.026 — safety signage. Four signal colors encoding urgency: red (danger), yellow (caution), green (safe), blue (mandatory action).

4BO Protective Green (#51653F). The canonical Soviet vehicle exterior color, used from the 1930s through the Cold War; based on Vallejo 70.609. It appears in soviet_military and as steppe’s middle stop.

The avant-garde palettes. lissitzky, popova, stepanova, and malevich are color-sampled from reproductions of specific Constructivist and Suprematist works (1916–1920). They are evocative of those works rather than authoritative color records. ```
